ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for tuna soup as a composite dish; A0CKP (tuna, cooked) used as closest defensible anchor. Composite dish preparation noted.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of tuna soup is approximately 250 g, representing a typical single-serve portion for lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Tuna Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tuna Soup (cr_id G00010431). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Fish and Vegetable Soup — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fish-based soups and mixed vegetable soups
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tuna soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous fish-based soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently report GI values in the 35–45 range for protein-rich, moderate-carbohydrate fish soups. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (40) × carbohydrate per serving (9.41 g/100g × 250 g serving = 23.5 g) / 100 = 9.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 402 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium offset (178 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as the sodium content of 402 mg/100 g approaches the borderline-unsuitable threshold and soup broth concentrates sodium further; avoid adding soy sauce or fish sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (16 g/100 g) with moderate carbohydrate (9.41 g/100 g) and low estimated GI (~40)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tuna soup is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high protein content slowing gastric emptying and moderating post-meal glucose response; monitor portion size and avoid adding starchy thickeners or noodles that would increase glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Tuna omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) are cardioprotective, but sodium at 402 mg/100 g is a concern for cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tuna provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) and lean protein; however, the sodium content of 402 mg/100 g warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and reduced-salt preparation or smaller portions of broth are recommended to limit daily sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from tuna are anti-inflammatory, but sodium at 402 mg/100 g may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tuna is a rich source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which reduce joint inflammation; however, sodium at 402 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling and reduced-salt preparation is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tuna Soup is a Western-style fish-based soup prepared by simmering tuna in a seasoned broth, typically with vegetables such as tomatoes, onions, and celery. The dish is commonly served in Singapore as a light lunch or dinner option. The soup has a savoury, mildly briny flavour with a tender fish texture and a clear-to-lightly-thickened broth. Nutritionally, tuna soup is protein-rich at 16 g per 100 g, providing essential amino acids and omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that support cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. The moderate carbohydrate content of 9.41 g/100 g and low estimated glycaemic index (~40) make it suitable for glycaemic management in individuals with type 2 diabetes. However, the sodium content of 402 mg/100 g is a clinical concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and reduced-salt preparation is recommended. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0 g/100 g. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore food service contexts and is gluten-free and dairy-free, making it accessible to individuals with common dietary restrictions. A standard serving is approximately 250 g (one bowl), providing approximately 468 kcal, 40 g protein, and 23 g carbohydrate.
common_user_queries: Is tuna soup good for diabetics?
Is tuna soup high in sodium?
Is tuna soup suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Tuna Soup (cr_id G00010431) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and available micronutrients are taken from this primary approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this tuna soup preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010431) as of 2026. saturated_fat_g estimated at 2.0 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for tuna-based soups (SR Legacy); this is consistent with the total fat of 9.26 g/100 g and the known fatty acid profile of tuna. sugar_g estimated at 1.5 g/100 g based on the carbohydrate composition of typical tuna soup preparations in USDA FoodData Central; HPB does not report sugar separately for this entry. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source (0.0 g/100 g reported). GI is an analogous estimate of 40 (low) from University of Sydney GI Database fish-based soup analogues; no direct GI measurement for tuna soup exists in the database. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis contained an internal contradiction (stating tuna is a rich omega-3 source while simultaneously marking suitable=false and claiming no positive anti-inflammatory factor is present); this has been corrected in health_context to reflect the established clinical evidence that tuna omega-3 fatty acids are anti-inflammatory. Cuisine is classified as Western per the input cultural metadata. This dish is served in Singapore and multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.
